tagline_green: Conditions are working. Go pull on something.
tagline_yellow: Climbable, but compromised — bring patience and a brush.
tagline_red: Stay off the rock for now.
crag_blurb: Compact, fast-draining Ruhrsandstein — dries quicker than soft sandstone, but still never climb it wet.
forecast_note: Best light expected during daylight ({start}:00–{end}:00), with the window when conditions are on.
outlook_window_good: then good until ~{label}, when rain returns
outlook_window_brief: only a brief window — rain back {label}
outlook_no_rain: No rain in the 7-day forecast — go climbing
reason_wet_inside: Rock still wet inside — climbing now breaks holds and damages the crag
reason_wet_inside_wait: Internal moisture lingers after the surface looks dry — wait for it to clear
reason_air_humid: Humid ({rh}% RH) — holds may feel greasy
footer_disclaimer: A hint, not a safety guarantee — always check the rock yourself.
method_lead: Every hour we pull the forecast for {name}, model how wet the rock is, score the climbing conditions from 0–100, and turn that into a light. It's tuned to the rock you actually climb on — {rock} — and the drying is real physics, not a fixed “wait N hours after rain”. It's a hint, not a safety guarantee.
method_rules_intro: Two hard rules can force a 🔴 on their own, no matter how nice the air is:
method_rule_wet_strong: The rock is still wet inside.
method_rule_wet_rest: For fragile rock like {rock}, climbing while the core is saturated snaps holds and damages the crag — even once the surface looks dry.
method_blend_intro: Otherwise we blend four factors into a 0–100 score, each weighted by how much it matters for friction on this rock:
method_thresholds_note: A would-be green is knocked down to yellow if the surface is still damp or rain is due within {h}h — you can climb, but it's compromised.
method_s2note: Every number here is specific to this rock — other stone behaves differently. Granite dries fast and is sound even when wet; limestone seeps; grit and sandstone are fragile.
method_s3intro: Rain doesn’t just “clear after a day”. We track two reservoirs of water in the rock, stepped hour by hour through the forecast:
method_surface_body: Water sitting on the rock — what makes holds slick. Dries quickly. Drives the dryness score.
method_core_body: Water soaked into the pores. Dries slowly — this is why {rock} stays unsafe for a day or more after the surface looks dry.
method_et0: Both drain in proportion to reference evapotranspiration (ET₀) — a standard index of how hard the air is pulling moisture off a surface, computed from the forecast’s sun, temperature, wind and humidity. So a warm, breezy day dries the rock far faster than a cold, still, damp one, and near freezing the drying nearly stops.
method_caveat_body: ET₀ is defined for a grass reference surface (FAO-56), not rock — we borrow it only as a proxy for evaporative demand. The rock-specific parts (how much rain soaks in, how slowly the core dries, “never climb it wet”) rest on porous-media evaporation physics and climbing-ethics guidance, not on the ET₀ standard itself.
method_eta_explain: The “🪨 likely dry around …” estimate isn’t a fixed delay — it’s the first hour our model reckons the rock is climbable again. We also look past it: if more rain is coming, we show when that dry window closes.
method_s4post: (free, no account). We fetch the past 3 days — so we know how wet the rock already is — plus 7 days ahead: temperature, humidity, wind, precipitation & probability, weather code, and FAO-56 ET₀.
method_limit_1: It’s a model with hand-tuned parameters, not measurements of the actual rock.
method_limit_2: Forecasts are wrong sometimes; shade, seepage lines and aspect vary across a crag.
method_limit_3: We only look back 3 days, so a very wet spell before that is invisible.
method_limit_4: Always check the rock yourself — if it’s damp, don’t climb it.
method_src_et0: ET₀ definition (the value we pull from Open-Meteo; a grass reference, used here only as a proxy)
method_src_sandstone: Wet sandstone is weaker (up to ~45% loss of compressive strength when saturated, most within hours)
method_src_ethic_tail: : don't climb grit & sandstone when wet.
